channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Pope Leo Warns of Risks From A.I. in 42,300-Word Encyclical
item.description: The document marks a powerful foray by the leader of the Roman Catholic Church into the debate about the misuse or overuse of artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Motoko Rich, Elisabetta Povoledo and Elizabeth Dias
item.media:description: Pope Leo XIV at the presentation of his first encyclical letter, “Magnifica Humanitas,” at the Vatican on Monday. In it, he outlined his desire to protect human dignity and agency in an age of artificial intelligence.

item.title: As A.I. Fever Rises in Silicon Valley, Pope Leo Has a Few Words
item.description: The American pope wants to take artificial intelligence down a notch. Is he challenging the tech companies, or will tech take over the papacy?
item.dc:creator: David Streitfeld

item.title: Pope Leo Has Released an Encyclical About A.I. Why Is That Important?
item.description: Leo XIV issued his thoughts about artificial intelligence in the modern world using a centuries-old form of papal communication called an encyclical.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo
item.media:description: Pope Leo has made artificial intelligence a central theme of his first year as pontiff.

item.title: Trump’s Pressure Had Little Effect on Iran’s Terms for a Peace Deal
item.description: President Trump had sought to force Tehran to accept American demands for a peace deal with a mixture of threats and military operations.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: For weeks, the Trump administration pursued a diplomatic agreement rather than returning to war in Iran.

item.title: Even After a Strait of Hormuz Deal, Moving 1,500 Ships Won’t Be Easy
item.description: An agreement to reopen the waterway would be followed by a complicated process of navigating a backlog of vessels stranded for nearly three months.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Companies will need to know how their ships will be prioritized if passage through the Strait of Hormuz is restored.

item.title: What Are the Abraham Accords, Trump’s 2020 Mideast Deals?
item.description: The diplomatic agreements normalized relations between Israel and several Arab countries. Now, as part of the peace talks with Iran, Mr. Trump wants other states to join.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim, Amelia Nierenberg and Tyler Pager
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel, far left, and President Trump with the foreign ministers of Bahrain and the United Arab Emirates during the signing of the Abraham Accords at the White House in 2020.

item.title: Absent Congressman Calls Allies, but Stops Short of Public Appearance
item.description: Republican officials said they have talked to Tom Kean Jr., who has been absent from Congress for weeks. His office said Mr. Kean will eventually explain his absence.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully
item.media:credit: Bryan Anselm for The New York Times
item.media:description: Although Republican officials have now reported hearing his voice, Tom Kean Jr.’s whereabouts for now remain a mystery.

item.title: Trump’s Self-Indulgent Streak Deepens G.O.P. Fears He Is Risking Losses in Midterms
item.description: “The stupid stuff is killing our chances,” said a retiring Republican senator.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher and Patricia Mazzei
item.category: White House East Wing and Ballroom (Washington, DC)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Donald Trump.

item.title: He Name-Drops Ocasio-Cortez in His Bid for Congress. She Doesn’t Talk About Him at All.
item.description: Saikat Chakrabarti, Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez’s former chief of staff, is running in San Francisco to succeed Representative Nancy Pelosi, whom Mr. Chakrabarti irked with an incendiary tweet seven years ago.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Saikat Chakrabarti is a Democratic candidate in the San Francisco district of Representative Nancy Pelosi, who is retiring.

item.title: Will Maureen Galindo’s ‘Zionists’ Comments Matter in Texas House Runoff Election?
item.description: Democratic leaders accused Maureen Galindo of antisemitism and are trying to stop her from winning the party’s primary in a contested U.S. House district.
item.dc:creator: Edgar Sandoval and J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Katina Zentz/San Antonio Express-News, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Maureen Galindo is a progressive with little political experience.

item.title: Were the Constitution’s Authors a Little Too Optimistic?
item.description: The nation’s founding document has a blind spot. Trump is making it visible.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Tom Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: The founders of the United States wanted a powerful chief executive, but one that was restrained in the use of that power.

item.title: At an LA Costco, Skateboarding and Learning About Loss
item.description: We’re all going to die. But first — just one more trick.
item.dc:creator: Conor Dougherty and Jake Michaels
item.media:description: At a time when people are lonely and disconnected, the Costco I skate at is a place where a bunch of 40- and 50-year-olds gather around low-stakes terrain, reconnecting.

item.title: Threat of Toxic Explosion in Orange County, Calif., Eliminated, Officials Say
item.description: Evacuation orders remained in place for more than 40,000 residents around Garden Grove, Calif., the location of an industrial site containing an unstable tank of toxic chemical.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales
item.media:description: An aerial view of water being sprayed onto a tank at GKN Aerospace in Garden Grove, Calif., on Saturday.

item.title: Artificial Intelligence Floods Court Dockets with Home-Brewed Lawsuits
item.description: For years, courts have welcomed cases brought by self-represented litigants. Now those plaintiffs have A.I., and their filings are consuming more and more bandwidth.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz and Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Jenn Ackerman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Donald Sauve works on stacks of legal documents as part of a pro se legal filing at a coffee shop in Mora, Minn.

item.title: A City in the Kill Zone
item.description: Little is left of Kostiantynivka, a city on the front lines. Rescuers risk their lives to ferry out the few who remain.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Hicks and Gaëlle Girbes

item.title: One-and-Done Heart Disease Prevention? Scientists Show It May Be Possible.
item.description: A single infusion of an experimental gene-editing drug seemed to reduce LDL long-term in a small trial. The results may point to something “curative,” one expert said.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:description: A colored transmission electron micrograph of low-density lipoprotein, or LDL, particles.

item.title: Everyone Knew Them on the Block. Then a Friday Ritual Ended in Tragedy.
item.description: The group of friends had used the same corner on the Upper West Side to unwind for years. By the end of the night, two people would be dead after a speeding S.U.V. drove into a crowd.
item.dc:creator: Nate Schweber
item.media:credit: Ryan Murphy for The New York Times
item.media:description: A memorial for Jason Negron and Michael Saint-Hilaire, two friends who were killed when a car drove onto a sidewalk in Morningside Heights.

item.title: Cubans Cook With Charcoal and Wood Fires to Survive During Energy Crisis
item.description: The U.S. oil blockade has left millions without cooking gas. In Santiago de Cuba, the cradle of the Cuban revolution, apartment tower residents resort to charcoal and firewood.
item.dc:creator: Ed Augustin and Lisette Poole González
item.media:description: Outside one of five 18 story apartment towers in Santiago de Cuba this month.

item.title: Blind Waymo Users Revel in the Joy of Riding Alone
item.description: Waymo has been an object of frustration to some in California. For visually impaired people, it can also bring a rare feeling of independence.
item.dc:creator: Sonia A. Rao and Rachel Bujalski
item.media:credit: Rachel Bujalski for The New York Times

item.title: Twenty Years After His Film, Al Gore Tweaks the Climate Script
item.description: Mr. Gore is still giving the slide show that “An Inconvenient Truth” was built around, but with changes that reflect a shift in the discussion of climate change.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan

item.title: Can Concrete Molds Revive Coral Reefs Ruined by Bombs and Climate Change?
item.description: In a devastated section of the Coral Triangle in the Pacific Ocean, a conservation group is trying to build an artificial reef.
item.dc:creator: Zunaira Saieed and Sirachai Arunrugstichai
item.media:description: Robin Philippo, the managing director of the Tropical Research and Conservation Center, installing an artificial reef structure near Pom Pom Island in Malaysia.

item.title: Summer’s Best Beach Reads
item.description: If you happen to be near a body of water (salt, fresh, chlorinated or otherwise), here are the books you’ll need.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Egan

item.title: This Memorial Day Starts a Summer That Is Longer Than Most
item.description: A calendar quirk brings more days of lemonade, glamping and difficult-to-open beach umbrellas to 2026.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:description: Hot time. Summer in the city.

item.title: New Waterside Getaways for the Summer
item.description: From the Hamptons to Majorca, here are six getaways that invite you to eat, drink, lounge and enjoy idyllic surroundings.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Rosenbloom

item.title: Iran Is Trolling Us and We’re Not Doing Anything About It
item.description: This is a novel form of propaganda.
item.dc:creator: Jessica Brandt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: How to Be Old
item.description: My advice for how to keep living.
item.dc:creator: Roger Rosenblatt

item.title: A Poem for Memorial Day
item.description: We remember those who gave their lives in service to the United States.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: In Arlington National Cemetery last week.

item.title: One of Madagascar’s Oldest Sacred Trees Is Slowly Dying
item.description: In a forest in Madagascar, the demise of a centuries-old baobab points to the fraying of a fragile ecosystem.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe

item.title: A Terrible Winter for Snow Heads Into a Bleak Summer of Drought
item.description: In the Desert and Mountain West, towns like Kearny, Ariz., are already warning residents they could soon run out of water, but rationing so far has been patchy.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy, Reis Thebault and Scott Dance

item.title: Toshifumi Suzuki, Who Made 7-Eleven a Giant in Japan, Dies at 93
item.description: He spent four decades building the convenience store chain into a cornerstone of daily life.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis
item.media:credit: Chika Ohshima/Kyodo News, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Toshifumi Suzuki in 2023. He led 7-Eleven Japan for decades, transforming it into a sprawling empire.

item.title: Brooklyn Co-op Divided Over Push to Boycott Israeli Products
item.description: The Park Slope Food Co-op, a socially conscious grocery store in a liberal neighborhood, has devolved into dysfunction ahead of a Tuesday vote on the proposed boycott.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy and Liam Stack
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Brandeis, Louis D, Center for Human Rights Under Law
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times
item.media:description: Some members of the Park Slope Food Co-op want the grocery store to stop selling about a dozen Israeli products as a response to the crisis in the Middle East.
